[JS Weird World] No.60 Strict Mode: Make JavaScript Stop Covering for Your Typos
JsWeirdWorld
JavaScript
JavaScript 全攻略:克服JS奇怪的部分

After the last few chapters, you've probably noticed how loose JavaScript can be. But if you want it to be stricter, that's totally doable. There's a single line you can add to flip JavaScript into strict mode — and it'll stop quietly covering for the kind of typos that cause nasty bugs.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/24/js-weird-world-59-typeof-instanceof/
[JS Weird World] No.59 typeof, instanceof, and Figuring Out What Something Actually Is

We already know JavaScript is a dynamically typed language. That lets us do some pretty special things, but it's also dangerous. Normally when we want to know a variable's type, we reach for typeof — but honestly that method isn't great. So this chapter walks through typeof and instanceof, and how to actually figure out what something is.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/24/js-weird-world-58-initialization/
[JS Weird World] No.58 Initialization
JavaScript Full Course: Conquering the Weird Parts

When you write object literals in JavaScript, it's really easy to slip up with a missing comma, a missing brace, or a typo like a semicolon where an equals sign should be. This post walks through those classic mistakes and why the syntax parser yells at you — which is all part of initialization.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/24/js-weird-world-57-es6-classes/
[JS Weird World] No.57 ES6 and Classes

This chapter covers ES6 (JavaScript EcmaScript 2015, aka EcmaScript 6) — specifically another way to create objects and set up prototypes. The class keyword is super popular in a lot of other languages, where you use it to define an object's methods and properties. But as we covered earlier, JavaScript doesn't actually have real classes. Let's see what ES6 classes really are under the hood.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/24/js-weird-world-56-object-create-prototypal-inheritance/
[JS Weird World] No.56 Object.create and Pure Prototypal Inheritance

We've already seen how to build objects with function constructors, and why they exist in the first place — to mimic languages that can't do prototypal inheritance. Those languages use `class` to define what an object does and `new` to create it, which is exactly what function constructors are imitating. But JavaScript has another way to build objects: `Object.create`. Let's look at it and think about pure prototypal inheritance along the way.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/23/js-weird-world-55-arrays-and-for-in/
[JS Weird World] No.55 A Dangerous Reminder: Arrays and for in
JavaScript Full Guide: Conquering the Weird Parts of JS

We've already seen some of the dangerous corners of the built-in function constructors. But arrays and for in have their own traps too. In this post I'll show why looping an array with for in can bite you, and why a plain for loop is the safer bet.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/23/js-weird-world-54-built-in-function-constructors/
[JS Weird World] No.54 A Little Danger Warning: Built-in Function Constructors

We've already talked about built-in function constructors in earlier chapters, but they come with some genuinely dangerous corners. They're concise, sure, but that conciseness hides traps. This chapter focuses on why built-in function constructors get sketchy when they're dealing with primitives, booleans, numbers, and strings.
/en/misc/2019/05/23/what-is-a-user-story/
What's a User Story?
Misc
User Story
User Stories

An interviewer asked me about User Stories and I realized I wasn't super clear on it — so I dug in and wrote a short note. A User Story captures a feature from the user's perspective ("As a [user], I want [something] so that [reason]") rather than from a database or implementation perspective. This post is the basics — the formula, a few real examples, and how it shifts how you think about building features.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/22/jsweirdworld-53-built-in-function-constructors/
[JS Weird World] No.53 Quick Reminder: The Built-in Function Constructors

What do you actually get back from new Number(3) — a primitive value, or an object? In this post we look at JavaScript's built-in function constructors (Number, String, Object) and how the methods on their prototypes really work.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/22/jsweirdworld-52-new-and-functions/
[JS Weird World] No.52 A Dangerous Little Reminder: `new` and Functions

What happens if you forget the `new` in front of a function constructor? `this` ends up pointing at `window`, the function returns `undefined`, and every prototype method becomes unreachable. This post explains why the industry convention is to capitalize the first letter of a constructor.
